But, important to highlight is that this business unit is expected to continue growing at a quick pace as enterprise spending isn't slowing thanks to many firms/governments continuing to digitalize their workspaces.

This is one of the reasons why I prefer to own Alphabet in my portfolio vs. Meta Platforms (META). Meta's business model is fully ad-dependent, an industry which is cyclical. Google is moving away from this fully exposed business model. On top of that, you get YouTube, which isn't the conventional "social media," and it alone could be valued around $500-600B.

AI Spending

Shortly, I want to touch base on Alphabet's AI spending and its implications on the earnings.

In 2025, the firm spent $91.5B in CapEx, with most going towards the build-up of AI infrastructure. In 2026, the firm is planning to spend $180B, basically a 2x increase. Only Amazon is planning to spend more in 2026.

Google is certainly a beneficiary of AI integration, and the accelerating revenue growth is a clear vote of confidence, but this spending will pressure margins in the future, especially via higher deprecation.

It's fair to say that while for firms like Meta Platforms the revealed budget of $125B sent shockwaves and pressured the stock price, the same hasn't happened to Alphabet. This suggests shareholders are seeing the tangible benefit of the AI spending/integration, but at the same point we need to ask, how much is enough?

If Alphabet indeed spends $180B in 2026, I need to see the firm's revenue growth be 20-25%. It's projected at ≈17%.

What becomes very clear from Q4 2025 is that we are starting to see Alphabet's operating margin contract. Just slightly, but it went from 32% in Q3 2025 to 31.6% in Q4.

In the past few years, Alphabet's operating margin always trailed Meta's, but we are starting to observe the same trend where the margin of both peers is declining due to the heavy investments in AI infrastructure and hiring of expensive AI talent. In 2026, this impact is expected to be even more pronounced, as expenses will outgrow revenue growth.

What troubles me a bit is that in full-year 2025, Alphabet's free cash flow was $73B, adj. for CapEx. If Alphabet follows through with the plan to spend as much as $180 in CapEx, free cash flow will with high probability be negative in 2026. On the flip side, the firm is still sitting on ≈$127B of cash.

Valuation

Alphabet's valuation at P/E 20x was dirt cheap during 2023-H1 2025, as many investors simply discarded the firm due to the lack of AI strategy and innovation. This was a perfect time for accumulation.

As you would expect, nothing could have been further from the truth, and the market swung like a pendulum. Shares skyrocketed in 2025, and the stock is now priced at a blended P/E of 27x. The S&P 500 is now priced at 22.3x - hence Alphabet trades at a premium here.

The firm's 15Y avg. valuation was 24-25x P/E. Over this duration, EPS grew at avg. rate of 19%.

The FactSet is now expecting the EPS growth will slow down significantly in 2026, driven by the massive increase in AI-related costs:

  • 2026: Expected EPS of $11.48E, 6% YoY growth
  • 2027: Expected EPS of $13.42E, 17% YoY growth
  • 2028: Expected EPS of $15.37E, 15% YoY growth.

Revenue growth in 2026 isn't expected to slow down; it's projected to be 17% (I would like to see 20-25% in light of the AI spending), but you see the divergence between revenue/EPS growth due to the ballooning costs. And the ballooned costs won't be a 1Y bug; they'll drag on, even if CapEx spending slows in the subsequent years, due to how depreciation works. This is the main risk I see with Google heading into 2026 (but other Big Tech as well).

From the graph below, you can see amidst the general negative market sentiment, the price/valuation has contracted from a P/E of 31x to the current 27x, but it isn't a cheap stock anymore per se. If Alphabet's valuation remains around 25-26x - as it did in the past, we could see 13% annual ROR, which is still very attractive, though.

Investor's Takeaway

Alphabet's stock was on a rollercoaster in the last 3 years.

The firm went from being discarded, with analysts citing a lack of AI strategy and falling behind in the race, to being praised for its strategy and now being crowned as "AI leader." That's the market. The mood shifts all the time.

Following Alphabet's release of Gemini 3, AI overviews, and overall strong Google Cloud growth, the company is on a track to keep accelerating its revenue growth in the next quarters. The issue, once again, is the spending. We are starting to see that with the $180B planned in CapEx for 2026, the top-line growth won't be enough to offset the decline in EPS growth as expenses will explode.

The shares aren't cheap anymore, per se, and trade now at a premium to the S&P 500 (SP500), but still offer decent upside into 2026 and beyond.
